ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CJW (Shrimps and prawns, coated and fried) is the closest defensible parent. No specific Ebi Fry entry exists in FoodEx2.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of Ebi Fry consists of 3 breaded deep-fried shrimp, weighing approximately 120 g in total.

nutrient_source.title: Shrimp, breaded and fried — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 15150)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for breaded fried shrimp (NDB 15150 / FDC 175177) used as the primary nutrient reference. Ebi Fry is the Japanese preparation of panko-breaded deep-fried shrimp, which is nutritionally analogous to the USDA breaded fried shrimp entry. Values are adapted from per-100 g USDA data. Panko coating is slightly lighter than Western breadcrumb coating; values are considered a close estimate. No HPB Singapore or FAO/INFOODS SEA entry exists specifically for Ebi Fry.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal, American Diabetes Association)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Ebi Fry in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 45 based on the analogous food category of breaded fried fish/seafood products with panko-style wheat breadcrumb coating. Panko-coated fried foods have a moderate carbohydrate content from the coating; the GI of white breadcrumbs is approximately 70–75, but the mixed food matrix (protein-rich shrimp, fat from frying) substantially lowers the effective GI of the composite dish. An estimate of GI 45 (low category, ≤55) is consistent with published mixed fried seafood dishes in Atkinson et al. 2008 and Brand-Miller et al. peer-reviewed GI tables. GL per serving = (45 × 16.9 × 1.20) / 100 = 9.1.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~292 mg per 100 g; ~350 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Ebi Fry to occasional consumption, as the sodium from seasoning and the breading contributes meaningfully to daily sodium targets; a 120 g serving provides approximately 350 mg sodium, representing about 15% of the 2,300 mg daily limit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load from panko coating with low estimated GI (~45)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Ebi Fry has a low estimated glycemic index (~45) due to the protein and fat matrix, but the panko coating contributes approximately 20 g of carbohydrate per serving; people with Type 2 Diabetes should account for this carbohydrate load within their meal plan and pair with non-starchy vegetables to moderate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol content (~151 mg per 100 g) and deep-frying fat absorption
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Ebi Fry is deep-fried and contains moderate saturated fat (~2.1 g per 100 g) and notable dietary cholesterol from shrimp (~181 mg per serving); individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit frequency of consumption and prefer grilled or steamed shrimp preparations when possible.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ebi Fry (エビフライ, Ebi Furai) is a classic Japanese Western-style (yōshoku) dish consisting of whole shrimp that are butterflied or left whole, coated in wheat flour, dipped in beaten egg, and encrusted in panko breadcrumbs before being deep-fried in oil until golden and crispy. The dish originated in Japan during the Meiji era as part of the yōshoku culinary tradition, which adapted Western cooking techniques to Japanese ingredients. The result is a shrimp with a light, airy, and exceptionally crunchy panko crust on the outside and a tender, juicy, sweet shrimp interior. Ebi Fry is commonly served with shredded cabbage, lemon wedges, and tonkatsu sauce or tartar sauce. It appears on the menus of Japanese family restaurants (famiresu), bento boxes, teishoku set meals, and is widely available across Japan and in Japanese restaurants globally, including Singapore. Nutritionally, a 120 g serving (approximately 3 pieces) provides around 290 kcal, 20 g protein, 14.5 g fat, and 20 g carbohydrate. The shrimp provides high-quality protein, vitamin B12, selenium, phosphorus, and iodine. The panko coating contributes carbohydrates and absorbs some frying oil, increasing fat content. Sodium is moderate at approximately 350 mg per serving. The estimated glycemic index is low (~45) due to the protein-fat matrix moderating glucose absorption. Clinically, Ebi Fry is a moderate-concern food for hypertension (sodium), Type 2 Diabetes (carbohydrate from coating), and cardiovascular disease (cholesterol and frying fat). It is not suitable for those with crustacean shellfish allergy, wheat/gluten allergy, or egg allergy.

What is the difference between Ebi Fry and tempura shrimp?
What sauce goes with Ebi Fry?
Is Ebi Fry high in cholesterol?
meal_context: field survey data Japanese restaurant order

ingredient_synonyms.panko: Japanese breadcrumbs, light breadcrumbs, crispy breadcrumbs

confidence_reason: No exact HPB Singapore, FAO/INFOODS SEA, or Japanese MEXT nutrient entry was found specifically for Ebi Fry as a composite dish. Nutrient values are adapted from the USDA SR Legacy entry for breaded fried shrimp (FDC 175177), which is the closest approved-source match. GI is an analogous estimate based on peer-reviewed mixed fried seafood data. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC 175177, breaded fried shrimp) as no exact Ebi Fry entry exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, FAO/INFOODS SEA, or ICMR-NIN databases. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source entry for this food. 3. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Ebi Fry was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. The estimate is based on published GI values for breaded fried seafood and mixed protein-fat-carbohydrate dishes in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care). 4. Vitamin C is reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero per USDA source; shrimp and breadcrumb matrix contains negligible vitamin C). 5. Trans fat is reported as 0.1 g per 100 g per USDA source; this is a trace amount from frying oil. 6. The input description references 'tempura batter' but Ebi Fry is canonically a panko-breaded dish, distinct from tempura (which uses a thin egg-flour batter). The canonical preparation has been used for nutrient estimation. 7. Halal status is flagged as likely true for plain shrimp and panko preparation, but restaurant-specific halal certification was not verified for The Oyster Bank - Funan.
